docs.title: System Dynamics with DisSModel
docs.text:
Welcome to the official documentation for the dissmodel-sysdyn package. This library is a specialized toolkit for building, simulating, and visualizing System Dynamics (SD) models using the Python programming language.

Try it Now: Interactive Demo

You can explore all the models in this package right in your browser! Visit our live demo on Hugging Face Spaces: dissmodel-sysdyn-demo

docs.title: What is System Dynamics?
docs.text: System Dynamics is a methodology and mathematical modeling technique used to understand, conceptualize, and analyze the behavior of complex systems over time. Originally developed by Professor Jay Forrester at MIT in the 1950s, it focuses on the internal structure of a system—how elements interact through feedback loops and delays—to explain nonlinear behavior.

docs.title: The Core Pillars
docs.text:
To model any system dynamic, we rely on three fundamental concepts:

  1. Stocks (Accumulators): These represent the state of the system at any given moment. They are the "bathtubs" of the system, accumulating flows over time (e.g., population, wealth, amount of water).
  2. Flows (Rates): These represent the activities that change the stocks. Flows are the "faucets" and "drains" (e.g., birth rate, interest accumulation, evaporation).
  3. Feedback Loops: The circular chain of cause and effect.
    • Reinforcing (+): Leads to exponential growth or collapse (e.g., interest on savings).
    • Balancing (-): Leads to stability and goal-seeking behavior (e.g., a thermostat).

docs.title: Why use dissmodel-sysdyn?
docs.text:
While many System Dynamics tools are purely visual (like Vensim or Stella), dissmodel-sysdyn brings the power of Computational Modeling to the Python ecosystem:

  • Pedagogical Clarity: Designed for students and researchers to see exactly how equations translate into code.
  • Scientific Rigor: Leverages established numerical integration methods (Euler) and scientific libraries (SciPy, NumPy).
  • Automatic Visualization: Integrated with the DisSModel core to provide real-time tracking and plotting of simulation variables.
  • Interoperability: Easily integrate your models with Jupyter Notebooks, Streamlit apps, or data science pipelines.

docs.title: Getting Started
docs.text:
If you are new to the package, we recommend the following path:

  1. Read the Architecture Overview to understand how we translate Forrester diagrams into Python classes.
  2. Explore the Tutorials for a hands-on laboratory experience with the SIR and Predator-Prey models.
  3. Use the Models Reference as a technical encyclopedia for the 14 pre-built models included in this library.

Theoretical Foundation

"The behavior of a system is determined by its structure." — This is the central tenet of System Dynamics. By modeling the structure of feedback loops, we can predict—and influence—the behavior of the world around us.

docs.title: Architecture Overview
docs.text: The dissmodel-sysdyn package provides a high-level framework for System Dynamics (SysDyn) modeling in Python. Inspired by the philosophy of TerraME, it allows researchers and students to translate Forrester diagrams directly into executable code using clear abstractions for Stocks, Flows, and Converters.

docs.title: Core Abstractions
docs.text: In dissmodel-sysdyn, the fundamental components of System Dynamics are mapped to Python's object-oriented primitives:

docs.title: 1. Stocks (Accumulators)
docs.text:
Stocks represent the state of the system at any given time. In our framework, Stocks are defined as instance attributes of a model class. They are initialized in the setup() method and updated in the execute() method.

Example

In a population model, self.population is a Stock that accumulates individuals over time.

docs.title: 2. Flows (Rates of Change)
docs.text:
Flows represent the rate at which Stocks change during a time step. Unlike some visual tools where Flows are explicit objects, dissmodel-sysdyn expresses Flows as intermediate variables or logic within the execute() method.

Mathematical Mapping

A flow is equivalent to the derivative dS/dt or the difference ΔS.

docs.title: 3. Converters and Parameters
docs.text: Converters (or auxiliary variables) and Parameters influence the rates of Flows. These are implemented as model attributes that are typically set during the setup() phase and remain constant or change according to their own internal logic.

docs.title: Numerical Integration and Time Discretization
docs.text: System Dynamics models are naturally described by Ordinary Differential Equations (ODEs). To solve these on a computer, we must discretize time.

docs.title: The Integration Loop
docs.text: The Environment class manages the simulation clock. At each time step (t -> t + dt), it calls the execute() method of all active models.

docs.title: Euler Method
docs.text:
Most models in this package employ the Forward Euler Method. The new value of a Stock is calculated by adding the net flow (Inflows - Outflows) multiplied by the time step to the current value:

S(t + dt) = S(t) + (Inflow - Outflow) * dt

In many discrete models within the package, dt is implicitly assumed to be 1, simplifying the update to: 

self.stock += inflow - outflow

docs.title: Framework Comparison: Radioactive Decay
docs.text: To illustrate the framework overhead versus the business logic, consider a simple radioactive decay model.

docs.title: Pure Python (Procedural)
docs.text:
amount = 100.0
decay_rate = 0.1
for t in range(50):
    decay = amount * decay_rate
    amount -= decay
    print(f"Time {t}: {amount}")

docs.title: dissmodel-sysdyn Framework
docs.text:
Using the framework adds structure, automatic plotting, and environment management.

from dissmodel.core import Model, Environment
from dissmodel.visualization import track_plot

@track_plot("Substance", "blue")
class Decay(Model):
    def setup(self, amount=100.0, rate=0.1):
        self.amount = amount  # Stock
        self.rate = rate      # Parameter

    def execute(self):
        # Flow logic
        decay = self.amount * self.rate 
        # Update rule (Euler)
        self.amount -= decay

# Running the simulation
env = Environment(end_time=50)
model = Decay()
env.run()

The framework allows you to focus on the Differential Equations and Model Logic while providing tools for complex orchestrations and visualizations.

docs.title: Step-by-Step Tutorials
docs.text: This guide provides practical, laboratory-style tutorials for the two most iconic models in System Dynamics: the SIR epidemiological model and the Lotka-Volterra Predator-Prey model.

docs.title: Tutorial 1: Mapping an Epidemic (SIR Model)
docs.text: In this laboratory, we will simulate the spread of a contagious disease through a population of 10,000 individuals.

docs.title: Step 1: Environment Setup
docs.text:
We define the time horizon for our simulation. Epidemiological events often unfold over weeks or months. Here, we set a 60-step duration.

from dissmodel.core import Environment
from dissmodel_sysdyn.models import SIR
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# Create the simulation environment
env = Environment(end_time=60)

docs.title: Step 2: Model Configuration
docs.text:
We instantiate the SIR model. We start with a nearly susceptible population and a small number of "Patient Zeros".

# Initialize the model with specific parameters
model = SIR(
    susceptible=9995, 
    infected=5, 
    recovered=0,
    contacts=10,      # High contact rate
    probability=0.1,   # 10% chance of transmission
    duration=5         # Disease lasts 5 steps
)

docs.title: Step 3: Run the Simulation
docs.text:
Execute the environment loop. The framework will call the model's execute() method at each step.

# Run the simulation
env.run()

docs.title: Step 4: Visualization
docs.text:
A System Dynamics model is best understood through Time Series. We will plot the evolution of all three compartments.

# Accessing history
plt.figure(figsize=(10, 6))
# Plot Susceptible (Green), Infected (Red), Recovered (Blue)
plt.plot(model.history["susceptible"], color='green', label='Susceptible')
plt.plot(model.history["infected"], color='red', label='Infected')
plt.plot(model.history["recovered"], color='blue', label='Recovered')

plt.title("SIR Model: Epidemic Evolution")
plt.xlabel("Time Steps")
plt.ylabel("Number of Individuals")
plt.legend()
plt.grid(True, linestyle='--', alpha=0.7)
plt.show()

docs.title: Tutorial 2: The Dance of Life and Death (Predator-Prey)
docs.text: Learn how to simulate the oscillatory relationship between wolves (predators) and moose (prey).

docs.title: Step 1: Initialize the Space
docs.text:
Set a longer time horizon to observe at least two full cycles of oscillation.

from dissmodel.core import Environment
from dissmodel_sysdyn.models import PredatorPrey
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

env = Environment(end_time=200)

docs.title: Step 2: Define Biological Parameters
docs.text:
The stability of the cycle depends on the balance between growth and death rates.

model = PredatorPrey(
    prey=1000, 
    predator=40,
    prey_growth=0.1,         # Fast prey reproduction
    prey_death_pred=0.002,   # Predation efficiency
    pred_death=0.1,          # Predator mortality
    pred_growth_kills=0.0001 # Conversion of prey to predator offspring
)

docs.title: Step 3: Execute the Cycle
docs.text:
Observe how the populations interact. As prey becomes abundant, predators thrive; then, over-predation leads to a prey crash, followed by a predator famine.

env.run()

docs.title: Step 4: Multi-Axis Visualization
docs.text:
Because prey and predator populations often have different orders of magnitude, we use contrasting colors and clear legends.

fig, ax1 = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 6))

# Plot Prey on primary axis (Green)
ax1.set_xlabel('Time Steps')
ax1.set_ylabel('Prey Population', color='green')
ax1.plot(model.history["prey"], color='green', label='Prey (Moose)')
ax1.tick_params(axis='y', labelcolor='green')

# Plot Predators on secondary axis (Red)
ax2 = ax1.twinx()
ax2.set_ylabel('Predator Population', color='red')
ax2.plot(model.history["predator"], color='red', label='Predator (Wolves)')
ax2.tick_params(axis='y', labelcolor='red')

plt.title("Lotka-Volterra: Predator-Prey Oscillations")
fig.tight_layout()
plt.grid(True, axis='x', linestyle=':', alpha=0.5)
plt.show()

Observation

Notice the phase shift: the predator population peaks shortly after the prey population reaches its maximum. This "lag" is a classic signature of negative feedback with delay.

docs.title: Reference Guide
docs.text: This reference guide provides a detailed technical description of each model available in the dissmodel-sysdyn package. Each entry explains the theoretical background, the underlying equations, and the Python implementation.

docs.title: Description
docs.text: The Chaotic Growth model simulates a single-species population using the discrete logistic map (quadratic map). It demonstrates how simple, deterministic rules can lead to complex, chaotic behavior. For growth rates near 4, trajectories become extremely sensitive to initial conditions.

docs.title: Stocks, Flows & Parameters
docs.text:
• Stocks:
    • pop: Normalized population in the interval (0, 1).
  • Parameters:
    • rate: Growth rate parameter (r). Influences the system's stability and chaos.

docs.title: Model Logic
docs.text:
The update rule follows the standard logistic map: P(t+1) = r * P(t) * (1 - P(t))

def execute(self) -> None:
    self.pop = self.rate * self.pop * (1.0 - self.pop)

docs.title: Full Usage Example
docs.text:
from dissmodel.core import Environment
from dissmodel_sysdyn.models import ChaoticGrowth

env = Environment(end_time=100)
model = ChaoticGrowth(pop=0.1, rate=3.9)
env.run()

docs.title: Description
docs.text: This model simulates the cooling of a hot beverage towards ambient temperature. It is a classic example of first-order negative feedback, where the rate of change is proportional to the difference between the stock and its target.

docs.title: Stocks, Flows & Parameters
docs.text:
• Stocks:
    • temperature: Current temperature of the beverage.
  • Parameters:
    • room_temperature: The ambient temperature (target).
    • cooling_rate: Proportionality constant (k).

docs.title: Model Logic
docs.text:
The temperature update at each step follows Newton's Law: T(t+1) = T(t) - k * (T(t) - T(room))

def execute(self) -> None:
    self.temperature -= self.cooling_rate * (self.temperature - self.room_temperature)

docs.title: Full Usage Example
docs.text:
from dissmodel.core import Environment
from dissmodel_sysdyn.models import Coffee

env = Environment(end_time=30)
coffee = Coffee(temperature=90, room_temperature=22, cooling_rate=0.15)
env.run()

docs.title: Description
docs.text: Based on the famous model by Watson and Lovelock, Daisyworld demonstrates how life can regulate a planet's environment. White and black daisies interact with solar luminosity through albedo feedback to buffer the planetary temperature.

docs.title: Stocks, Flows & Parameters
docs.text:
• Stocks:
    • white_area: Area covered by white daisies (high albedo).
    • black_area: Area covered by black daisies (low albedo).
    • empty_area: Bare soil.
  • Parameters:
    • sun_luminosity: Fractional solar luminosity.
    • white_albedo, black_albedo, soil_albedo: Reflectivity values.
    • decay_rate: Mortality rate of daisies.

docs.title: Model Logic
docs.text:
The model calculates a global average temperature (Tave) based on the planet's aggregate albedo. Local temperatures for each daisy type then determine their growth rates. Planet_Albedo = Sum(Area_i * Albedo_i) Area(t+1) = Area(t) + Area(t) * (Growth - Decay)

def execute(self) -> None:
    pa = self._planet_albedo()
    self.ave_temp = _planet_temp(self.sun_luminosity, pa)

    temp_white = _local_temp(self.ave_temp, pa, self.white_albedo)
    white_growth = _daisy_growth_rate(temp_white) * self.empty_area
    self.white_area += self.white_area * (white_growth - self.decay_rate)
    # ... same for black area

docs.title: Full Usage Example
docs.text:
from dissmodel.core import Environment
from dissmodel_sysdyn.models import Daisyworld

env = Environment(end_time=200)
model = Daisyworld(sun_luminosity=1.0)
env.run()

docs.title: Description
docs.text: The Homeostasis model illustrates a system reaching a stable equilibrium through a constant inflow and a rate-proportional negative feedback. It is a fundamental building block for self-regulating systems.

docs.title: Stocks, Flows & Parameters
docs.text:
• Stocks:
    • stock: The accumulated value.
  • Parameters:
    • gain: Fixed increment added each step (Inflow).
    • rate: Proportional feedback coefficient (Outflow regulator).

docs.title: Model Logic
docs.text:
S(t+1) = S(t) + g + r * S(t) Where g is the gain and r is the negative rate.

def execute(self) -> None:
    self.stock += self.gain + self.rate * self.stock

docs.title: Full Usage Example
docs.text:
from dissmodel.core import Environment
from dissmodel_sysdyn.models import Homeostasis

env = Environment(end_time=50)
model = Homeostasis(stock=10, gain=5, rate=-0.2)
env.run()

docs.title: Description
docs.text: This model extends simple exponential growth by adding a carrying capacity. As the population grows, the available resources decrease, slowing down the growth rate until the system reaches a stable limit.

docs.title: Stocks, Flows & Parameters
docs.text:
• Stocks:
    • pop: Population size.
  • Parameters:
    • rate: Intrinsic growth rate (r).
    • capacity: Environmental carrying capacity (K).

docs.title: Model Logic
docs.text:
The update equation is the discrete logistic growth model: P(t+1) = P(t) + P(t) * r * (1 - P(t)/K)

def execute(self) -> None:
    self.pop += self.pop * self.rate * (1.0 - self.pop / self.capacity)

docs.title: Full Usage Example
docs.text:
from dissmodel.core import Environment
from dissmodel_sysdyn.models import LimitedGrowth

env = Environment(end_time=100)
model = LimitedGrowth(pop=10, rate=0.2, capacity=1000)
env.run()

docs.title: Description
docs.text: A system of three coupled ordinary differential equations originally derived from atmospheric convection. It is a classic example of deterministic chaos and the "Butterfly Effect".

docs.title: Stocks, Flows & Parameters
docs.text:
• Stocks: x, y, z (State variables).
  • Parameters:
    • sigma, rho, beta: System coefficients.
    • delta: Integration time step (dt).

docs.title: Model Logic
docs.text:
The system uses Forward Euler integration: dx/dt = sigma * (y - x) dy/dt = x * (rho - z) - y dz/dt = x * y - beta * z

def execute(self) -> None:
    dx = self.sigma * (self.y - self.x)
    dy = self.x * (self.rho - self.z) - self.y
    dz = self.x * self.y - self.beta * self.z

    self.x += self.delta * dx
    self.y += self.delta * dy
    self.z += self.delta * dz

docs.title: Full Usage Example
docs.text:
from dissmodel.core import Environment
from dissmodel_sysdyn.models import Lorenz

env = Environment(end_time=5000)
model = Lorenz()
env.run()

docs.title: Description
docs.text: A water-balance model for Mono Lake, based on Ford's Modelling the Environment. It accounts for precipitation, runoff, evaporation, and human export to track lake volume and elevation.

docs.title: Stocks, Flows & Parameters
docs.text:
• Stocks:
    • water_in_lake: Lake volume in KAF.
  • Converters:
    • level: Lake elevation (derived from volume).
  • Parameters:
    • prec_rate, evap_rate, runoff, export.

docs.title: Model Logic
docs.text:
Change_V = (Precipitation + Runoff + In) - (Evaporation + Export + Out)

def execute(self) -> None:
    self.water_in_lake += self._total_input() - self._total_output()
    self.level = float(_water_elevation(self.water_in_lake))

docs.title: Full Usage Example
docs.text:
from dissmodel.core import Environment
from dissmodel_sysdyn.models import MonoLake

env = Environment(end_time=50)
model = MonoLake(export=150)
env.run()

docs.title: Description
docs.text: Simple exponential growth model with a variable growth rate. It can simulate accelerating or decelerating growth.

docs.title: Stocks, Flows & Parameters
docs.text:
• Stocks:
    • population: Number of individuals.
    • growth: Current growth rate.
  • Parameters:
    • growth_change: Multiplier for the growth rate.

docs.title: Model Logic
docs.text:
P(t+1) = P(t) * (1 + r(t)) r(t+1) = r(t) * c

def execute(self) -> None:
    self.population *= (1 + self.growth)
    self.growth *= self.growth_change

docs.title: Full Usage Example
docs.text:
from dissmodel.core import Environment
from dissmodel_sysdyn.models import PopulationGrowth

env = Environment(end_time=20)
model = PopulationGrowth(population=100, growth=0.05, growth_change=1.1)
env.run()

docs.title: Description
docs.text: Models the interaction between a prey population and a predator population. The system exhibits cyclic oscillations where predators lag behind prey peaks.

docs.title: Stocks, Flows & Parameters
docs.text:
• Stocks: prey, predator.
  • Parameters:
    • prey_growth, prey_death_pred, pred_death, pred_growth_kills.

docs.title: Model Logic
docs.text:
P(t+1) = P(t) + rP * P(t) - dPP * P(t) * Q(t) Q(t+1) = Q(t) - dQ * Q(t) + gQ * P(t) * Q(t)

def execute(self) -> None:
    self.prey += (self.prey_growth * self.prey - self.prey_death_pred * self.prey * self.predator)
    self.predator += (-self.pred_death * self.predator + self.pred_growth_kills * self.prey * self.predator)

docs.title: Full Usage Example
docs.text:
from dissmodel.core import Environment
from dissmodel_sysdyn.models import PredatorPrey

env = Environment(end_time=200)
model = PredatorPrey()
env.run()

docs.title: Description
docs.text: A stochastic model where a value moves up or down with a specific probability. Demonstrates drift and diffusion.

docs.title: Stocks, Flows & Parameters
docs.text:
• Stocks: value.
  • Parameters:
    • prob: Probability of an upward step.
    • seed: Random number generator seed.

docs.title: Model Logic
docs.text:
V(t+1) = V(t) + noise Where noise is +1 with probability p and -1 with probability 1-p.

def execute(self) -> None:
    rng = object.__getattribute__(self, "_rng")
    if rng.random() <= self.prob:
        self.value += 1.0
    else:
        self.value -= 1.0

docs.title: Full Usage Example
docs.text:
from dissmodel.core import Environment
from dissmodel_sysdyn.models import RandomWalk

env = Environment(end_time=100)
model = RandomWalk(prob=0.55, seed=42)
env.run()

docs.title: Description
docs.text: Simulates indoor temperature regulation against a time-varying outdoor climate. Uses a thermostat logic (inertia) and thermal loss to the environment.

docs.title: Stocks, Flows & Parameters
docs.text:
• Stocks: inside.
  • Converters: outside (Time-varying).
  • Parameters: temp_set, thermal_inertia, loss_to_outside.

docs.title: Model Logic
docs.text:
Delta_in = Theta * (Tset - Tin) Delta_out = Lambda * (Tin - Tout) Tin(t+1) = Tin + Delta_in - Delta_out

def execute(self) -> None:
    t = self.env.now()
    self.outside = self._climate(t)
    inflow = self.thermal_inertia * (self.temp_set - self.inside)
    outflow = self.loss_to_outside * (self.inside - self.outside)
    self.inside += inflow - outflow

docs.title: Full Usage Example
docs.text:
from dissmodel.core import Environment
from dissmodel_sysdyn.models import RoomTemperature

env = Environment(end_time=24)
model = RoomTemperature()
env.run()

docs.title: Description
docs.text: Tracks the spread of a disease through Susceptible, Infected, and Recovered compartments. It is the foundation of modern mathematical epidemiology.

docs.title: Stocks, Flows & Parameters
docs.text:
• Stocks: susceptible, infected, recovered.
  • Parameters: contacts, probability, duration.

docs.title: Model Logic
docs.text:
Delta_I = I * (contacts * probability) * (S/N) Delta_R = I / duration

def execute(self) -> None:
    total = self.susceptible + self.infected + self.recovered
    alpha = self.contacts * self.probability
    new_infected = self.infected * alpha * (self.susceptible / total)
    new_recovered = self.infected / self.duration

    self.susceptible -= new_infected
    self.infected += new_infected - new_recovered
    self.recovered += new_recovered

docs.title: Full Usage Example
docs.text:
from dissmodel.core import Environment
from dissmodel_sysdyn.models import SIR

env = Environment(end_time=60)
sir = SIR(infected=5, probability=0.3)
env.run()

docs.title: Description
docs.text: A fundamental stock-and-flow model representing water levels in a tub with constant drainage and periodic inflow.

docs.title: Stocks, Flows & Parameters
docs.text:
• Stocks: water.
  • Parameters: out_flow, in_flow, in_period.

docs.title: Model Logic
docs.text:
Water level is updated every step and clamped at zero. W(t+1) = max(0, W(t) - Outflow + Inflow_periodic)

def execute(self) -> None:
    self._step += 1
    self.water -= self.out_flow
    if self.water < 0.0: self.water = 0.0
    if self._step % self.in_period == 0:
        self.water += self.in_flow

docs.title: Full Usage Example
docs.text:
from dissmodel.core import Environment
from dissmodel_sysdyn.models import Tub

env = Environment(end_time=50)
tub = Tub(water=100, out_flow=2, in_flow=50, in_period=20)
env.run()

docs.title: Description
docs.text: Models yeast proliferation in a closed culture using a logistic equation with an explicit hard cap at the carrying capacity.

docs.title: Stocks, Flows & Parameters
docs.text:
• Stocks: cells.
  • Parameters: capacity, rate.

docs.title: Model Logic
docs.text:
C(t+1) = C(t) + C(t) * r * (1 - C(t)/K) The result is explicitly clamped to K.

def execute(self) -> None:
    self.cells += self.cells * self.rate * (1.0 - self.cells / self.capacity)
    if self.cells > self.capacity:
        self.cells = self.capacity

docs.title: Full Usage Example
docs.text:
from dissmodel.core import Environment
from dissmodel_sysdyn.models import Yeast

env = Environment(end_time=15)
yeast = Yeast()
env.run()
